data.title: Zscaler Vs. Netskope: The Battle For The AI Cybersecurity Edge
data.description:
I have been breaking down cybersecurity companies for some time now. I wrote an article on Palo Alto Networks (PANW) after they acquired CyberArk (CYBR). I’ve also written about Okta (OKTA), and not only gave it a Buy rating, but I actually bought the stock myself. Not long ago, I also talked about Check Point Software (CHKP), and the stock is down by about 7.5% since then. I really like that company and think it has an edge, so if you are looking for a good, progressing cybersecurity company at a discount, Check Point may be the one to check out.

Very recently, I compared CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Fortinet (FTNT) and gave CrowdStrike a Hold, while rating Fortinet a Buy because the former is more expensive, and may consolidate after the gains of the last 2 years. Today, I want to take a look at two other players that are very different in size, operations, and cash flows, and in how they are perceived by the market. They are very different in valuations, but they both position themselves as AI for cybersecurity, or cybersecurity against AI threats.

The two companies I want to break down today are Netskope, Inc. (NTSK), a cybersecurity stock that had its IPO only in September of last year, 2025, for $19 per share. Right now, the stock trades at $15.70, creating a nice discount even compared to the IPO price. The second is Zscaler, Inc. (ZS), a known company that has actually traded higher in 2025 while most of its competitors have been in the red.

One of the reasons I am so consistent in breaking cybersecurity names is that, in my opinion, this is one of the sectors that will benefit the most from the growing AI efforts we see today. However, those companies probably will not grow on those initiatives in 2026. To me, cybersecurity is a sector that is currently beaten down and trending lower after enormous gains a few years ago, which is exactly why I want to look at this industry right now while it's not at its peak. When we actually face threats from AI, the industry will already be surging. So, now is an interesting moment to start buying cybersecurity stocks, even though they may trend down further. That is okay for me because I am willing to wait. If you are not like that, if you are looking for explosive, fast growth, maybe cybersecurity is not the sector for you yet.

Looking at Grand View Research, you can see that the faster growth, especially in services and software, starts more actively from 2028 to 2033. In general, this market is presumed to expand at almost 12% compound annual growth rate from 2026 to 2033.

The main problem with cybersecurity stocks is the competition. We have so many companies competing for the same clients that it’s hard to understand how all of them are still standing. This includes Fortinet, Check Point, Palo Alto Networks, CrowdStrike, Zscaler, SentinelOne (S), Rubrik (RBRK), Netskope, Okta, Tenable (TENB), Qualys (QLYS), Varonis (VRNS), and others. Don't forget there are also private companies. This is likely one reason why stocks like SentinelOne are down by 35% just this year; the company shrinks in growth, and the valuation remains relatively expensive because of the lower “E” in the P/E ratio.

Zscaler

Zscaler creates AI agents that help with cybersecurity using Generative AI. The company tries to change legacy firewalls, which is an interesting line of business given that Wedbush recently noted companies like Fortinet and Check Point might have problems due to DRAM shortages. Zscaler can benefit from weakness in those areas because, instead of legacy firewalls protecting network access, Zscaler allows customers to secure access on an app-by-app basis. They call this the "Zero Trust Exchange."

AI initiatives of the company are in anomaly detection using a massive amount of data. They are processing over 0.5 trillion transactions daily, and specifically for AI, they are already securing over 90 billion AI/ML transactions per month.

Zscaler uses AI to automate human jobs, basically threat hunting. They recently acquired companies like Red Canary and SPLX to build out these AI agents. If you don’t think AI will replace humans, you might have questions about Zscaler. They rely on AI agents powered by LLMs, and there is a lot of talk that LLMs are basically just calculations without true intelligence. If we view LLMs as machines transforming words into patterns of numbers, they will likely never fully substitute people. If the buzz around LLMs goes away, Zscaler could be hurt by this narrative.

However, the company is growing. A quarter ago, they had 21% year-over-year revenue growth, but this quarter, they accelerated to 26% growth with $788 million in revenue, beating their guidance. But what really impressed me was their efficiency. They are operating at what they call the "Rule of 78," that is, their revenue growth (26%) combined with their massive free cash flow margin (52%). This beats CrowdStrike and Fortinet by a wide margin of about 20%, especially in the most recent quarter.

Their AI is already paying off. "AI Security" business alone has already surpassed $400 million in Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR), growing over 80% year-over-year. They even hit their FISCAL targets for this segment three quarters early. On top of that, their new Z-Flex program (which lets customers swap modules) generated over $175 million in contract value just this quarter.

Comparing this with Netskope is not easy because Zscaler is a much "older" company in terms of market presence, having gone public in 2018. Zscaler is more than 72% down from its 5-year average valuations, but it is still not cheap. It could definitely trade below the $200 level again.

Netskope

Netskope is a very interesting company because it doesn't work the same way. While Zscaler is building "Agents," Netskope uses machine learning to categorize data. So basically, Zscaler adds AI on top of its existing operation, while Netskope is being built for it. I like this edge of theirs, because even if we imagine a world with no LLMs (no ChatGPT or Gemini), businesses will still use Dropbox, Slack, Google Drive, and other software. Netskope is built to secure those SaaS apps by recognizing data.

So instead of just seeing a "JPG file," Netskope goes inside the image to see if it contains any sensitive info that the company doesn't want to leak. It also checks where you are sending it. For example, it might allow an upload to a corporate Google Drive but block the same file if you try to upload it to your personal email. They already have over 1,000 customers specifically to protect GenAI interactions, proving that there is a demand for this approach.

Netskope is the secure and fast on-ramp to everything enterprises access from the Internet, including websites, cloud and AI to their own infrastructure, private apps, LLMs and data centers. We are helping thousands of enterprises modernize and operate safely in both cloud and hybrid environments.

Then there is their infrastructure, NewEdge, which is a private cloud network that puts compute power at every data center. They realized that AI agents need a fast "highway" to operate effectively, so when machines talk to machines, there is almost no latency, and this speed is very important.

Netskope has a major partnership with Microsoft (MSFT), specifically for integrations in Microsoft Purview and Copilot, and it looks like it has been paying off handsomely. In their Q3, they reported $184 million in revenue, up 33% year-over-year, and their Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) hit $754 million.

This strong execution resulted in a 34% year-over-year increase in annual recurring revenue, reaching $754 million and Q3 revenue growth of 33% to $184 million.

Their Net Retention Rate is at 118%, and 53% of their customers are now using more than 4 products, meaning they are sticky. As a newly public company, they are still running at a loss on a GAAP basis, but they are getting better on cash flow. They generated $11 million in positive free cash flow this quarter, which is a great sign for a young public company. With about $1.15 billion in cash on the balance sheet and a market cap of around $6 billion, it offers a real chance for substantial upside.

We built Netskope to scale, and that is reflected in our ability to generate $11 million in free cash flow in Q3 as well as improved operating margin by 11 percentage points year-over-year and deliver free cash flow margin of 6%.

Conclusion

If you are looking for stocks that could grow a lot, Netskope may be one of those companies. I would probably choose Netskope over Zscaler at the moment because of the narrative and the valuation. Netskope has a forward Enterprise Value to Sales of 8, while Zscaler is at almost 10. Zscaler is definitely more expensive, but with their "Rule of 78" performance, they have earned a premium.

That doesn't mean I wouldn't invest in Zscaler. It is a great company, and I know companies and startups here in Israel that work closely with them and consider them one of the best. I would definitely choose Zscaler as a stock to hold as AI grows and cybersecurity demands increase, but at the moment, I would rather start my position in Netskope.

data.title: Affiliated Managers Group: Up 30% Since We Last Liked The Baby Bonds, We Are Buying More
data.description:
We turn our attention to one of our favorite companies, Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. (AMG). In our previous article, we looked at the company and the yield potential of its baby bonds and possible profit scenarios. Today, we return to the topic, since things are looking even better. AMG's common stock has risen by as much as 30%:

AMG also outperforms its peers by total return, as you can see in the 5-year total return comparison below.

Everything looks beautiful for AMG, even compared to the overall market:

Now let's get into the details.

AMG Basic Information

  • Total Assets (Q3 2025): around $8.9 billion
  • Current Market Capitalization: close to $ 8.78 billion
  • Total Debt: $2.54 billion
  • 2025 investments:
  • Performance:

AMG's 5-year total return on NAV is close to 23.33%, and its 10-year total return is close to 9.15%.

  • Credit Rating:

AMG falls into the “investment grade” category with its credit ratings from the major credit agencies:

  • News:

WEST PALM BEACH, Fla., Nov. 14, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- AMG, a strategic partner to leading independent investment management firms globally, today announced an adjustment to the conversion rate of its 5.15% junior convertible trust preferred securities due 2037 (the “junior convertible securities”). The conversion rate adjustment is being made in accordance with the indenture governing the conversion of the junior convertible securities as a result of the quarterly cash dividends paid by AMG on common shares since the fourth quarter of 2019 through the previously announced quarterly cash dividend with an ex-dividend date of November 13, 2025 to be paid on November 28, 2025. Effective immediately, the conversion rate is adjusted from 0.2558 common shares per $50.00 junior convertible security to 0.2582 common shares per $50.00 junior convertible security, equivalent to an adjusted conversion price of $193.6483 per common share, compared to the prior conversion price of $195.4652 per common share.

Baby Bonds

AMG has four baby bonds: (MGR), (MGRB), (MGRD), and (MGRE):

The bonds are rated as "investment-grade" by the major credit agencies. Moody's rates them with a credit score of "Baa1", and S&P rates them with "BBB-". The bonds are high-duration, all with close to 32-38 years to maturity. MGR and MGRB's call dates have passed, while MGRD and MGRE are still not callable. All four currently are traded below par, and have a yield to worst of above 7.2%.

We are facing a lot of questions about yield calculations, and we calculate them using the XIRR function in Google Sheets, which is supposed to be the most precise way when dealing with ex-dividend dates. It is a bit more work than just finding a random bond yield calculator, but at least we try to ensure the data is accurate.

The most attractive for us is MGRD - the bond is with the highest yields to worst of close to 7.34% at the moment. It was issued on 07/08/2021 with 4.20% coupon rate. The spread at its IPO date to the 20-year Treasury yield was 2.36%, and currently it is close to 2.58% - this is one of the few cases, as the current trend is for spreads to narrow. MGRD has around 35.7 years to maturity, with a maturity date on 09/30/2061. Its call date is on 09/30/2026 with 0.71 years to call. This is what the potential yields to worst and prices look like:

Comparison with peers

Below is shown a comparison between the AMG's baby bonds and their peers in the finance sector - high-duration baby bonds that traded below par. We can see AMG's bonds are among the highest yields, especially MGRD:

And here we separate those that have similar credit ratings like our bonds - MGRD is at the top:

And below we have chosen over-the-counter bonds that have ratings between BAA1 and BAA3 by Moody's, and the highest yield to maturity in this group is close to 6.3%. In such a high duration, 1.04% yield spread will give us more than 14% capital appreciation potential, if the market treats AMG's baby bonds similarly to the overall bond market.

AMG's OTC bonds

Below, you can see the over-the-counter bonds of AMG:

They are rated higher than the baby bonds, with an A3 credit rating from Moody’s, and they also offer lower yields, close to 2% spread to MGRD.

Conclusion

AMG is a stable, investment-grade issuer, and MGRD offers a rare combination of high investment-grade credit, high comparable yield to worst, and significant upside potential – making it a clear “buy” in the long-term baby bond segment.

data.title: Navan: Using AI To Do Real Work With Significant Benefits For Users And Shareholders
data.description:
Navan: A major disruptor

Navan (NAVN) is a fairly recent IPO-it went public at the end of October 2025 at $25/share. Despite a strong quarter that was announced last month, with results well above expectations and increased guidance, the shares are selling 30% below the IPO price with a remarkably compressed valuation. The company has emerged to challenge SAP's (SAP) Concur as the leading solution for business travel and expense management. It has a significant technology advantage with better functionality and a more user friendly implementation. It competes in a large space and has continued to acquire some substantial users. What's most probably driven the share price is that Navan is an enterprise software company, and investors are chary of making commitments in that space-on a seemingly irrational basis but one that has persisted for about 6 months at this point.

In late December and early January, one of the VC investors in Navan, Andreessen Horowitz, already a major holder of the shares, has been consistently adding to its position buying several million shares at current prices. At this point, Andreessen, through its various funds and through the holding of a director own more than 10% of the shares. It is quite unusual to see VCs buy more shares post-IPO but I assume that the valuation and the reaction of the shares to the earnings release and guidance increase has driven that decision. I have also initiated a position in the shares.

I am recommending the purchase of Navan shares at this price and at this time. That said, the shares are perceived as a "risk-on" commitment and are obviously part of the enterprise software space although revenue mostly do not come from the cost of subscription seats but come instead from travel bookings in the form of commissions as well as from its customers who pay based on a cost per trip or on the value of their bookings. Navan is apparently not perceived as part of the so-called "AI trade" at this point, although in my opinion, its solutions are at the current limit of what can be done with AI technology in terms of a consumer-facing app.

Navan was created to offer business users an AI based platform that can serve as a virtual travel concierge, an engine to optimize travel spend, and as a payment platform for travel expenses. It has a unified platform and a massive data estate that provide it with technology and functional advantages when compared to the offering from its principle competitor, Concur as well as compared to American Express (AXP).

At this point, most of the improvements in functionality are more about further industry disruption by Navan as it sells its unified platform to the largest enterprises. That platform includes an emphasis on Ava, a GenAi virtual assistant, its Unified "SuperApp' which puts all of the functionality in the category from trip booking, credit card usage and expense reporting and payments into a single platform. It is rapidly expanding its eco-system including partnerships with Sage Intacct and Uber for Business. It is now offering a Pro version of its app through a high-touch version with a human interface staffed by travel consultants at Reed & Mackay, a Navan subsidiary.

Navan's revenue model is based on several flows. There is a freemium version in which revenue is generated by commissions from travel providers. There is an enterprise version which costs $25-$40/month. The company offers specialized support tiers including those that include access to Ava and Pro.

Most of the company's revenues come from usage based commissions and fees-the company gets commissions from airlines, hotels and other travel services suppliers. Last quarter usage based revenue was 92% of the total and grew 29% year on year. Revenues grew by about 14% sequentially-some of that growth is apparently seasonal and the company is projecting negative sequential growth this quarter consistent with results in the prior two years. Bookings volume growth accelerated to 40% last quarter, compared to 34% in the prior sequential quarter. Payment volume growth has been slower than bookings volume growth and is a focus area for the company. Navan offers a card and when its users make their payments using that card, interchange fees are generated.

To summarize Navan is disrupting its space by offering a unified platform with many specific features that rely on AI and this is resonating strongly with users. The company maintains that users who adopt the platform for all of their travel bookings can save an average of 15% on travel expenses through optimizing bookings procedures and preventing out-of-policy bookings. This writer is glad he no longer is a road warrior subjected to Navan's policy enforcement procedures-no Four Seasons Hotels, and a limited ability to try out multi-starred restaurants.

Navan Back Story and how it can displace SAP/Concur

Concur was the first significant company in the travel management/accounts payable space. After a lengthy gestation period, it was growing rapidly when acquired with revenue growth reported at 29%. SAP bought Concur in 2014 for about $8.3 billion, and EV/S of about 12.4X based on LTM revenues or more like 8X based on then current revenues.

While SAP doesn't report Concur revenues specifically, most recently its revenues were thought to be in the $5 billion range and 3rd party analysis suggested that its market share was most recently near 50%. While Concur offered a compelling and unique value proposition at the time of its purchase by SAP and still has a strong payback for users, with the advent of Navan it is facing, for the first time, a solution that from a functional and ease of use standpoint has significant advantages and which has demonstrated paybacks that make a compelling case for its adoption.

There are a number of very specific benefits that Navan has been able to offer to potential customers that have enabled the company to gain significant enterprise users over the past couple of years. Navan first started to offer a business expense management system in 2020.

The initial set of capabilities launched at that time included smart corporate cards with built-in policy controls. This enabled automated reconciliation and eliminated the need for employees to file expense reports. And in turn, this led to rapid reimbursement, almost always between 24-48 hours.

A couple of years ago, the company introduced Navan Connect, a major milestone for the company which enabled users to use the features of Navan, while continuing to use their existing corporate and Visa cards. As the link here shows, that was the missing piece that enabled Navan to start competing successfully for major corporate accounts.

Around the same time the company introduced Ava, the company's virtual travel assistant. The technology is based on a customized LLM that has been acquired from OpenAI. Ava handles most basic travel requirements such as bookings, and processes refunds and cancellations. It offers personalized recommendations and anticipates the requirements of travelers. What is important about Ava is that it takes needful actions rather than waiting for a traveler to ask for a particular reservation and understands the needs of many travelers so it can put together a complete trip including transportation, hotels, rent-a-car requirements and other specific hospitality needs.

Besides the benefits to travelers, Ava is able to act as a data analyst and it can be tasked to proactively identify savings opportunities. At the moment, Ava can handle up to 5 legs of a flight itinerary with multiple booking classes that conform to company policies. It is context aware in that if one trip segment is unavailable, it can adjust the entire trip to make it "work" within defined parameters. This 5 leg capability incorporating optimized costs and scheduling apparently, according to the linked article has been one of the most difficult bits of code to design and implement. At the moment, competitors do not have an equivalent capability although Amex GBT Egencia is said to be closest in terms of functionality.

A typical Navan use case these days includes a unified travel booking with automated policy guardrails and a Navan Reward incentive if the employee chooses more cost-effective hotel than allowed by corporate policy. It also includes payment functionality and spend analysis. If a flight is delayed or cancelled the technology or a human agent can rebook the next available flight. There is a "duty of care" app which tracks the location of all travelling employees in order to comply with implied corporate requirements. Travel expense data is automatically passed to ERP systems such as NetSuite-from Oracle (ORCL), Sage and Workday. The data analytics functionality is often used as a part of the negotiation to obtain volume discounts from travel providers such as airlines and hotel chains.

It has proven difficult on some occasions for all Navan users to obtain the holistic functionality described above. The company is using genAI (its Ava) to plug some gaps and its is using what is called New Distribution Capability to insure that direct to carrier pricing and the most transparent booking options are shown. The apps may seem simple but the reality is quite different as there are so many issues such as the pricing of hotels during a convention and the availability of hotels that are available on consumer sites.

Many 3rd party observers believe that Navan has the most complete travel management system currently available on a single, unified platform. There are certainly alternatives that are particularly useful for businesses looking for a platform that goes beyond travel expense management.

At the end of the day, Navan and Concur are the two major horses in this race and Concur has a far larger market share than Navan. Third parties and customer threads suggest that most users find Navan easier to use and to have a desirable user interface that is focused on mobile deployment. The biggest advantage is that at least these days, Concur is very siloed and requires multiple products such as Travel, Expense, Request and 3rd party management solutions to match what Navan can provide on a unified platform.

It may seem ironic, but Navan appears to be far ahead in the use of its intelligent agent technology, Ava, than what is offered by SAP. To an extent, SAP appears to have underinvested in Concur functionality and used it as a cash cow since it has been successful simply by being part of the SAP eco-system. That said, Concur has launched its own AI-powered bookings agent last October based on the Joule Co-pilot technology. It won't be part of a unified platform such as that which Navan offers, but will start to fill a significant technology gap. Several significant components of the functionality of Joule will not be available for a few more months. Needless to say, Navan management was asked about this during its earnings conference call. This was the response from the CEO:

We're seeing companies talk about using AI to attract travel. And for us, we also feel very comfortable about our moat, right? Anybody can make an itinerary using AI, but not everyone can make the AI into an actual booking and into an actual experience. You need the whole integrated platform to do that. So we feel very comfortable about our competitive positioning overall, not just in legacy and enterprise, but also relative to new entrants and new opportunities to take share.

Yes, maybe I can take it. I think that the old model of like connecting stitching together a lot of things, taking a booking tool like Comcare (Concur) and then a travel agency and to try to kind of connect them together is so antiquated. You basically -- you start to search for something in Comcare (Concur), then you're finding yourself calling an agent. And in the era that people are expecting for everything to be online for -- to see machine learning to see AI kind of really driving efficiency, making sure that the experience is great, you're finding yourself there with a completely different model calling an agent. So I think it's becoming completely irrelevant and comparing it to what we are doing in Navan is like it's night and day. So if the question, are we worried about it or do I even care about it, the answer is no.

I am a believer in hubris, so I would have avoided that kind of answer. But the reality is what it is, and Concur has been able to grow at mid-teen rates without matching Navan features and benefits.I haven't personally used Navan and it isn't likely that I will. I have used Concur. And perhaps of more relevance, so have friends and family. There is much to be said for having a unified platform with a mobile first interface. It can be frustrating to use Concur to book any kind of complicated trip.

In writing these kinds of articles, I am always thinking about moats and competition. I acknowledge that I am sometimes not the most adept when it comes to using on-line apps, but from my personal experience I think at the least Concur needs a new and far more intuitive User Interface and probably needs to be rewritten to offer an integrated approach to travel management requirements. I think many readers who use Concur in their jobs will acknowledge how frustrating it can be to use because of rigidities and lack of consideration for achieving a positive user experience. And despite recent upgrades by Concur to use its Joule technology in this space, rewriting the UI or replacing the code base with something more modern is highly unlikely to happen in the foreseeable future. I say that because there is simply no reason for SAP to make a massive investment here-Concur's growth has apparently remained in the mid-teens and Concur hasn't lost significant market share.

This is a very dynamic space and lots of the competition will be based on product releases encompassing a set of features that incorporate solutions for user pain points. While SAP is introducing its features through its Joule agent, Navan recently introduced Edge-it was actually announced as part of the earnings conference call presentation. Users seem to appreciate it and Navan gets somewhat higher grades in user satisfaction surveys. In addition to Edge, the company recently introduced something called New Distribution Capability. Again, this space is based on lots of features-but in this case the functionality advantage seems substantial in that the company can connect directly to airline inventor providing more inventory and more agile servicing.

Edge is for frequent travelers and high-end clients. It is, as the name implies, the edge in terms of what is possible at this time in terms of a completely automated travel experience. It uses Navn's AI to book an entire trip including flights, hotels, ground transport and even excursions and meals-it provides sdomething of a concierge experience notifying hotels of late check-ins, for example and it can self-heal itineraries that get upended because of flight delays and other unplanned problems. It "knows" the preferences of the traveler and uses those to provide specific booking to accommodate that preference. The software is supposed to avoid the frustrations travelers have when dealing with humans and other travel management systems.

According to Navan, Edge can book a trip in 5-7 minutes, a 70% time saving compared to manual and legacy technologies; when used across an enterprise, it can save 16% over a year of the cost of travel expense and it has particularly high customer satisfaction scores.

That said, even Edge has its pain points including occasional pricing consistency issues, the occasional inability to use high-tier award status and an inability to respond to "Act of God" issues when there are travel emergencies such as the recent Caribbean flight disruptions during the Venezuelan invasion of January 3rd.

Navan's competitive issue is mainly that it is competing with SAP. It is far less well known and regardless of its connectors to the SAP ecosystem, many large SAP users will want to stick to a single vendor offering which has the potential to mitigate the economic advantages of switching to Navan.

Navan's market share growth

One of the reasons to buy Navan shares is that it is gaining share in a large and growing market. In 2023, it was estimated that Concur had a market share of at least 50% and some analysis showed its market share to be as great as 63%. At that time, Navan was thought to have a market share of 1.7%. Since that time, Navan's revenue run rate has more than doubled, while the CAGR for its market space is probably in the mid-teens percent.

Navan has recently secured some major new reference customers. Visa (V) went live with Navan last quarter-the deal encompasses all of Visa's employees. Unilever (UL), Adobe (ADBE), Engie (a French multinational utility) and Axel Springer/Frasers have all become Navan customers recently. Citibank (C) is a customer and a partner. The company also has acquired DoorDash (DASH) and Duolingo as customers. In the tech space, Navan is used by OpenAI, Anthropic, Databricks and Stripe.

Almost all of these wins are competitive displacements against Concur and American Express. The users who are migrating simply do not like solutions based on stitching together tools-that is true both of the enterprisers but also the end-users who often deride antiquated technology when commenting on sites such as Reddit.

American Express and Concur have announced a major strategic alliance that will integrate their data and software. And there are significant potential savings to be had from the use of a unified platform to manage travel expenses across an enterprise.

While forecasting market share changes without all of the data to do trend analysis is going to be as much a guess as anything else, the reference customers that the company has acquired bode well, I believe, for a continued positive evolution for Navan in terms of market share. I think the issue for Navan in terms of percentage revenue growth is balancing its market share opportunity with its need to reach and sustain profitability and free cash flow margins.

As mentioned, the company provided a deliberately conservative forecast for the current quarter. It has not yet provided a forecast for the next fiscal year, 2027. Consensus estimates for the period are for revenue growth of 22.6%. Based on the current sales momentum, the opportunity to improve payment revenues, and the traction from new products such as Edge, I believe that Navan is likely to maintain a CAGR in the mid-20% range with the potential for stronger growth than that, especially if the macro economy remains in a positive state.

Business Model-Still a work in progress

The company was non-GAAP profitable last quarter with non-GAAP operating margins of 13% up from 4% in the year-earlier period. It has had positive non-GAAP operating margins in 3 of the last 4 quarters. The company has forecast a full year non-GAAP operating income of about 3%. Free cash flow margins will probably be negative by a similar percentage for the current fiscal year but are forecast to be positive for FY '27, i.e. the fiscal year that starts 2/1/26.

The company's non-GAAP gross margins have continued to increase steadily and were 74% last quarter up from 72% in the year earlier period. In Q3, non-GAAP opex rose by 17% year on year vs. 29% growth in revenues. One of the major drivers in gross margin improvement is the use of Ava to deflect the need for manual intervention in the booking process. Last quarter, Ava deflected 54% of inquiries and that number continues to ramp.

Going forward, I expect the Edge product to have a positive impact on gross margins. Edge customers, almost by definition, are going to have much higher than average bookings per user than average. Edge is designed to appeal to travel-heavy users with lots of complex bookings requirements. And the payments component of the business, i.e. the use of the Navan card, which is expected to start to show more robust growth going forward now that navan has available resources to fund usage, is also likely to be a significant margin tailwind.

Overall, the company has forecast 22 points of margin regression sequentially. In the year earlier period the margin regression was 12 points sequentially. The company is deliberately setting margin and revenue targets it expects to exceed.

The fourth quarter for us, our fiscal year is seasonally lower. And when we think about our guidance, we take -- we are taking a prudent approach. And you know, probably because you all have encouraged us to build a track record and credibility early in this public company cycle and that we'll continue to kind of remind you of the seasonality in our business, the usage-based revenue in our business and all of the trends kind of taking place in travel as well. So we're going to try to be prudent and conservative and continue to prove out this durable growth model.

I would be surprised to see margins regress by anything like 22 points this quarter. As part of the modelling suggestions presented by the CFO during the call was her suggestion to look at the Navan Business Travel Index. The latest index showed 20% growth while Navan booking revenue grew by 40%. Given the economic macros being presented in the last few weeks, I would be very surprised if the index didn't continue to show strong growth with Navan booking revenue growth exceeding the growth in the index. I think a reasonable forecast for revenue in this current quarter would be in the range of $170-$175 million, or revenue growth of around 30%, similar to the prior quarter. This would bring full year revenue to $695 million. A $14 million revenue upside to revenue, and 85% incremental operating margins would take the projected non-GAAP operating margin loss for the quarter to $5 million of positive non-GAAP operating income, or about 3%, with expected non-GAAAP EPS of $0.02 compared to a current 1st Call consensus estimate for non-GAAP EPS of loss of $0.28.

Currently the 1st Call consensus projects a non-GAAP loss of $0.47/share in the next fiscal year with revenue growth of 22.6%. I think both of those estimates will prove too low. In fact, given that the company has already projected positive free cash flow for the new fiscal year, it is hard to imagine that it will not also have positive operating margins and positive EPS. My model calls for revenue growth in the mid-high 20% range. I used 27% in my model and that takes projected revenues to $885 million. I think non-GAAP operating margins will wind up at 6%, with operating income of $50-$55 million. Given the company has a current net cash balance of about $850 million, I am projecting net other income of $25 million and pre-tax income of $80 million.

The company has plenty of NOL's but these days, many companies choose to accrue some kind of taxes even though they don't pay them. So, I have used a 20% potential tax rate which leaves reported non-GAAP income of $65 million. I have used an implied share count of 255 million, and that takes my EPS forecast for FY '27 to about $0.25. Navan has little in the way of deferred revenue given that most of its revenue comes from usage charges. This might change a bit going forward as the company sells multi-year, multi-seat contracts to large enterprises.

Navan's Valuation

Navan currently has an enterprise value of about $3.75 billion and is selling 30% below its IPO price. I am projecting the company's EV/S at around 4.2X. This is a very compressed valuation-it is well below average on a relative basis and really below average based on almost any other valuation metric. I usually consider projected free cash flow, CAGR and EV/S in looking at valuation. Looking at those metrics specifically puts the shares more than 35% below average, with a CAGR estimate of 27%.

I have mentioned earlier in this article the current negative sentiment with regards to the valuation of enterprise software companies. While that is probably a factor in Navan's current valuation, its revenue model is not based on seats to any significant degree, and the company's Cognition and Edge offerings are-at least to me-the apotheosis of what users can actually do with AI these days in a consumer facing app.

I only wish this app had been available when I had to travel frequently though I am left to wonder if the software could ever develop a relationship with travel providers and secure hard to get reservations and car category and room upgrades. Something like that is perhaps not as farfetched as it might seem; it is not beyond belief that Navan's AI will someday be trained to message travel providers with the business it is generating and in turn, ask for some special benefits outside of what it generally available in return.

I don't want to pretend to know when the sector rotation away from high growth enterprise software companies will end. I believe it will simply because the theory regarding AI agents destroying the demand for enterprise software is so off base. In the case of Navan specifically, its offering is entirely based on autonomous AI agents and could exist in no other way.

I rarely try to make a price target forecast simply because the targets are essentially based on the performance of similar companies and their valuation. Currently, the average analyst price target for the shares is a bit over $25. This price target is based on revenue growth and earnings that are well below the metrics I believe to be more likely. Presumably, if my forecasts regarding revenue growth and free cash flow bear out, the shares would have a valuation substantially greater than the current analyst average price target.

Navan Management

Navan's CEO and co-founder is Ariel Cohen. He is basically focused on the company's growth. He is partnered with Ilan Twig, the company's other co-founder and now Navan's Chief Technology officer. These men previously co-founded StreamOnce a business that was subsequently acquired by Jive. Both men worked at Hewlett-Packard (HPQ) earlier in their career. Mr. Twig also worked at RockMelt, a social media company acquired by Yahoo. The company's president is Michael Sindicich who has operating responsibility for the company's travel and expense platform. His role is basically that of leading the sales activity. Ofer Ben-David is the COO. Offer was previously the chief product officer at Tenable (TENB) and was the VP of central engineering at VMware.

Reed & MacKay is a business acquired by Navan 4 years ago. Its revenues are bit less than 15% of the company's total revenues. It provides more personalized, high-touch travel and event management capabilities than Navn's standard offering. It is now led by Nina Herold who had been Navan's COO. The strategy is to use the Reed & MacKay capabilities as a wedge to enter large potential Navan customers by providing white glove and large event management service. Ms. Herold had previously worked at Uber (UBER) as that company's product operations manager.

Risks to the investment thesis

I think the biggest risk to the investment thesis for Navan is its need to continue to take market share-mainly from SAP/Concur but also from American Express. I have laid out the case as to why this should happen, but Concur is fighting back. At this time, while Concur has introduced Joule, its own AI assistant, it still lags many functional abilities of Navan's integrated platform. Competition will be back and forth, but I believe that Navan has a current advantage and is most likely to retain that advantage over coming years. To an extent, this relates to an entrepreneurial management which has a complete focus on this space and a technology background to make the right development choices to attract larger enterprises to its platform.

Travel expenses are cyclical. In a recession, travel budgets are almost the first things to be constrained. Of course there are no current signs of a recession and the latest Navan Business Travel shows growth of 20%. But Navan would certainly suffer in a recessionary business environment.

Execution is going to be a key risk element. The company's Edge is designed to appeal to senior executives in large enterprises who take complex trips and who have very specific requirements. Making sure that all of the advertised functions work all of the time in just about every circumstance will be key for the company's strategy to gain share.

The company has an impressive roster of leaders with relevant experience. Like all smaller companies battling much larger and resource heavy rivals, the need for strong leadership is especially significant and any departures from the founding group would be greeted by investors with significant disapprobation.

The company's CFO recently departed and the company needs to name a high profile individual who can gain the confidence of investors and can grow the finance organization.

Finally, the last 6 months have seen substantial sector rotation away from high growth software companies. Sentiment is what it is and it doesn't have to be logical to have its effect. In my view this is currently the single greatest risk to the investment thesis.

The case to buy Navan shares now

Navan is a recent IPO; it went public last October at a price of $25/share and is now trading about 30% below that level. Subsequent to the IPO, the company's first reported quarter was well above prior expectations with growth in travel bookings of 40%.

The company is a leader in the travel/expense management software, a category in which it competes with Concur/SAP and American Express. In my opinion, it offers significant functional advantages based on its AI implementation, although Concur is certainly active in its attempts to match Navan's capabilities with the introduction of Joule AI agents.

These days, Navan has the most advanced functionality in the space based on 3rd party commentary of actual users and its unified platform resonates with many large customers. It appears to be winning significant market share and the space itself is very healthy with industry statistics of 20% year on year growth.

Unusually, Andreessen Horowitz, the leading VC for this company has been acquiring additional shares in the last 2 months. The company's valuation, based on a mid-20% CAGR and its current EV/S of just greater than 4X is significantly below average. It has already been able to generate positive free cash and it has forecast a full year positive free cash flow margins for FY '27.

The shares have been pressured by the sector rotation away from software since the time of its IPO. I think the basis for the rotation which is grounded, apparently on the thought of AI causing a negative growth inflection for the enterprise software space is way off base. In any event, Navan gets its revenues mostly from travel booking commissions, rather than from seats. In fact, its AI which has led to the Cognition and Edge offerings, is, in fact, the key to the company's success.

I have been acquiring shares gradually and expect to continue to do so.

data.title: Illinois Tool Works: Growth Acceleration As Macro Headwinds Ease, PLS Drag Fades, And Self-Help Initiatives Strengthen
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Illinois Tool Works Inc. (ITW) is poised for revenue growth acceleration as macro headwinds ease and company-specific initiatives gain traction. A reversal in the interest rate cycle should help drive demand across the company’s end markets like automotive, residential, and light commercial construction. Further, the company’s focus on Customer Back Innovation (CBI) should continue to support share gains, particularly in Automotive OEM, where ITW is outperforming global vehicle builds through higher content per vehicle and rapid growth in EV platform. The company should also benefit from less drag from Product Line Simplification (PLS) initiatives in the coming quarters. Besides organic growth, the company has a strong balance sheet to pursue bolt-on M&As.

On the margin front, operating leverage on higher volumes and improving mix from product line simplification initiatives should benefit margins. The margins should also benefit from enterprise initiatives, including sourcing optimization, productivity improvement, and 80/20 portfolio optimization. Additionally, customer-backed innovation should help the company command higher pricing. In terms of valuation, the stock is trading at a discount to its historical averages. Given the good growth prospects and lower than historical valuation, I have a buy rating on the stock.

Recent Financial Performance

I last covered ITW in November 2024. At that time, the company was posting sales decline and I was optimistic about its return to growth. While the macro environment has remained tough since then, the company has returned to growth, helped by good execution.

In Q3 2025, ITW reported a 2% year-over-year revenue growth (organic growth +1%, FX +2 percentage points, product line simplification initiatives -1 percentage point.) Segment-wise, Automotive OEMs was the strongest performer with revenue growth of 7% on a reported basis and 5% on an organic basis. Food Equipment, Welding, and Specialty Products also helped, and each reported 3% Y/Y growth. However, Test and Measurement reported flat year-over-year revenues, with organic sales declining 1% due to softer demand for capital equipment. Polymers and Fluids reported revenue decline of 2% year-over-year, with organic revenues declining 3% primarily due to tough comparisons in the Polymer business. Construction Products revenue declined 1% year-over-year, with organic revenues declining 2%.

On the margin front, Operating Margin was up 90 bps year-over-year to 27.4%. Enterprise Initiatives contributed 140 bps to Margin Expansion. Further pricing and supply chain actions contributed to margin growth and helped more than offset tariff impacts.

Outlook

Moving forward, I am optimistic about ITW's growth prospects. While ITW is executing well, the broader macro headwinds particularly in construction and some industrial markets have impacted its results. However, with the Federal Reserve reversing interest rates, I expect the company's sales to further accelerate in the coming quarters.

The Federal Reserve has started cutting rates and I am expecting further cuts with Powell's term ending in May and Trump likely to pick-up someone more amenable to rate cuts as the next chair. The company has good exposure to residential and light commercial construction which could benefit as the interest rate cycle reverses. In addition, industrial capex and equipment spending should also pick up as the interest rate cycle reverses. This should help drive recovery in the company's test and measurement business, and polymers and fluids business. Certain parts of automotive OEM business, which are exposed to legacy automakers, should also benefit.

Further, I expect the company to continue executing well and gain share through customer back innovation. Customer back innovation is expected to contribute 2.3 to 2.5 percentage points to growth in 2025 and management expects its contribution to grow to 3 percentage points annually by 2030. One of the areas where the company has done really well in terms of customer back innovation is automotive OEM. This business is outperforming the global vehicle builds by 200 to 300 basis points driven by content per vehicle gains, innovation-led product differentiation and rapid growth in EV platforms.

The company is growing its EV-related revenues, helped by increasing penetration with Chinese OEMs. EV adoption coupled with increasing content penetration should continue to help the company outperform global automotive builds moving forward. In addition, as discussed earlier, interest cycle reversal should also help drive demand in this market.

The company should also benefit from less drag from product line simplification initiatives moving forward. The company is undergoing product line simplification to exit low-margin, low-growth categories. In 2025, this is expected to be an approximately 1% drag for the company. However, as the company continues to make progress in terms of divesting less strategic product lines, the incremental drag from PLS should reduce. In the steady state, management is expecting PLS drag to be closer to 50 basis points vs. the 100 basis point impact it saw last year. So, in the coming years, I believe PLS should become less of a drag which should help improve the company's overall organic growth.

Overall, when we think about end-market recovery helped by interest rate cycle reversal, customer-backed innovation’s contribution improving and PLS drag decreasing, ITW is well positioned for good organic growth moving forward.

On the margin front, the company should benefit from operating leverage as the revenue outlook is positive. ITW has done a good job in terms of cost-cutting through its enterprise initiatives and portfolio simplification, so it should show good leverage on incremental volumes. The company's incremental margins have reached around 65% in the recent quarter vs. a long-term average of 35-40%. This should help drive disproportionate earnings growth as revenues accelerate moving forward.

The company should also continue to benefit from its cost-cutting initiatives under the company's enterprise initiatives. Management is targeting approximately 125 basis points of annual margin expansion driven by sourcing optimization, productivity improvement, and 80/20 portfolio optimization.

Another good driver is product line simplification, where the company is exiting low-margin, less-differentiated product lines. This should improve the overall margin mix.

In addition, customer-backed innovation should help the company command higher pricing, due to differentiated functionality, application-specific design, and IP protection. As CBI-driven products continue to become a larger portion of overall revenue base, it should help structural improvement in pricing.

The company has also done a good job in terms of offsetting tariff-related headwinds through pricing. Also, almost 93% of the company's production occurs locally in end markets, which gives the company insulation against tariff volatility. However, if tariffs were to increase further in the coming period, the company would be able to offset its impact through price increases and local manufacturing.

So I remain optimistic about the company's margin prospects driven by good volume leverage, ongoing cost-cutting initiatives, portfolio simplification, and innovation-driven mix improvement.

In addition to good organic growth and margin improvement prospects, the company also has a strong balance sheet with its net leverage of around 1.75x EBITDA (Net debt = $8,018 mn, TTM EBITDA =$4,593 mn). This gives the company optionality to pursue bolt-on M&As to further add to organic growth and margin improvement initiatives.

Valuation

ITW is trading at 22.77x FY26 consensus EPS estimate of $11.28 and 21.16x FY27 consensus EPS estimate of $12.13. This is a discount versus its five-year average forward P/E of 24.88x.

I see a good chance of the multiple re-rating to historical levels as the company's growth re-accelerates in FY26 and the company also continues to see good margin improvement. The company's business mix has improved over the past several years with its divesting low growth, low margin businesses through product line simplification initiative while focusing on customer-backed innovation which has improved growth and margin profile.

However, macro headwinds in certain end markets and PLS has impacted the company’s growth and prevented full impact of these initiatives to show up in the reported numbers. With macros improving helped by interest rate cuts and PLS drag moderating, result in accelerating growth and earnings. The company also has a healthy balance sheet with net leverage of ~1.75x which provides it optionality for growth through bolt-on M&As. Further, ITW stock offers a reasonable dividend yield of approximately ~2.5%. Even if we assume the stocks valuation multiple remains at the current levels, ITW’s high single-digit EPS growth over the coming years coupled with ~2.5% dividend yield should result in approximately 10% total return for investors. So, I find this reward-to-risk attractive at current levels and rate this stock a buy.

Risks

My thesis anticipates a reversal in the interest rate cycle to support recovery in the company’s end markets, including residential and light commercial construction. If interest rate cuts are delayed or smaller in magnitude than anticipated, the recovery in demand may not happen as expected. This could impact the company’s financial performance.

Another area to monitor will be the inorganic growth through acquisitions. ITW also relies on bolt-on acquisitions to complement its organic growth efforts. There are risks associated with M&As like potential overpayment for assets, integration issues, or not realizing the intended synergies.

Takeaway

ITW has good growth potential moving forward. The revenue growth should benefit from the interest rate cycle reversal, customer back innovation, decreasing product line simplification drag, and inorganic growth prospects from M&As. The margins should benefit from operating leverage, product line simplification actions, and cost-cutting initiatives. The valuation is also lower than historical. Given the good growth prospects and lower than historical valuation, I rate ITW stock a buy.

data.title: Quantum Computing: Plenty Of Cash, Still Waiting On Traction
data.description:
Over the last few months, I’ve looked at Quantum Computing Inc. (NASDAQ:QUBT) as just another quantum stock that’s just riding high on speculation and in a similar boat as BTQ Technologies, which I’ve covered before. For one, they both have tiny revenue bases. They also have very heavy operating losses, and they spend as if there’s a much bigger business to sustain all of this within the short term.

However, I think that’s where the similarities end, and what has pulled me into covering QUBT is its balance sheet and recent acquisitions. The company has raised an extraordinary amount of capital relative to where its business is today, so it is not a survival or liquidity story. For me, the real question is whether management can turn years of R&D, photonics know-how, and technical validation into a viable commercial engine. That’s a different kind of risk, and although I’m not ready to be constructive on the stock yet, I’d like to take a closer look at it today.

What QUBT Does: An Overview Of The Company

QUBT operates under one banner, but its business is really a blend of three activities that are still lumped together, at least for now.

First, it builds photonics-based optimization systems under its Dirac platform. These are not general-purpose quantum computers. They are purpose-built systems that aim to improve optimization workloads, with the pitch that they can accelerate certain classes of problems while running at room temperature.

It is also building thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) photonic chip capabilities, and it is already operating a commercial foundry in Tempe, Arizona, that serves external customers. I think this part gets a bit less attention because it doesn’t carry the same quantum buzzwords, but it is the piece that can eventually support a more repeatable business model.

Finally, QUBT earns most of its revenue today from services, which include R&D contracts, pilots, and custom engagements, often tied to government agencies and early commercial users.

I’d say that the company’s USP is its focus on photonics technology, and I’m watching how it performs while searching for a durable commercialization path.

Product Stack, Customers, And How Monetization Is Supposed To Work

Seeing as QUBT’s products and customers are basically inseparable at this stage, I treat them as one story.

On the Dirac side, the company targets optimization workloads that show up in logistics, scheduling, imaging, and security-adjacent use cases. QUBT is now offering cloud access to these systems, and this is important because it opens the door to product-oriented revenue rather than pure services. That said, we’re still at the early stage of adoption, and the evidence base is thin.

Conversely, it looks like the company’s foundry and photonic chip effort is where the business model can change meaningfully. Thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) is becoming increasingly relevant for photonics applications in AI infrastructure, datacom, and sensing, and this is a big market opportunity by any metric. QUBT’s own website says the market size for the technology could reach $2 billion by 2029, compared to today’s value of around $200 million. Other research papers I’ve seen put the figure a bit higher, although I did see one that said it could reach $11 billion by 2033. I doubt the latter because of the discrepancy between the figures, but clearly, there’s a lot of money to be made.

There are very few commercial-scale TFLN foundries today, especially outside of China, and QUBT operates the only TFLN fab that can produce 150mm wafers in the US, so that is another major strategic advantage. If QUBT can establish itself as a credible supplier, even at a modest scale, it can move from one-off pilots to repeat purchase behavior. That is what the income statement needs, and I cannot overstate how important the foundry is to QUBT’s story moving forward. Before it opened the foundry, a large part of its revenue came from services. Now, though, it can at least start to sell something repeatable. We can see some signs of this with the numbers over the last year or so.

Revenue progression (FY24 → FY25 (9M))

Period

Revenue ($000)

Commentary

FY2024

373

Almost entirely services and custom work

Q1 FY25

Q2 FY25

Q3 FY25

9M FY25

Shows direction, not scale

Q3 2025 was a standout, and that’s because it was the first quarter where the revenue reflected:

  • increased activity on custom hardware and R&D services, and

  • the beginning of revenue recognition tied to cloud-based access to Dirac systems

It is a transition, to be clear, but the crucial thing is that we’re seeing signs of progress.

Following on from that is the customer mix today, which reflects how early this is. If you go through the company’s earnings filings, you’ll see that government agencies and research institutions dominate, although there are a small number of early commercial buyers. Still, that mix comes with long cycles, uneven purchase timing, and lumpy revenue. It also explains why services still dominate. I suppose it’s a tradeoff that the company has to make, though. Those institutions are the only ones with the funds and patience to spend and wait for a developing technology like this.

However, if we just look back to last month, December 2025, it’s clear that QUBT wants to speed things up. At the time, it struck a deal to acquire Luminar Semiconductor, Inc. for $110 million in cash, and it expects to close in January 2026 pending bankruptcy court approval. In my view, that’s a sign that the company is no longer satisfied with waiting for organic commercialization to gradually arrive. It wants an operating photonics footprint, IP, and an existing top engineering team.

Then, just yesterday, January 12, 2026, it added another layer by becoming the stalking horse bidder for select remaining assets of Luminar Technologies’ LiDAR business, with an initial bid of about $22 million, subject to auction and court approval. In my opinion, this bid for LiDAR assets changes the risk profile, because it pushes QUBT into a carve-out and auction dynamic where the outcomes are not fully under its control. For example, it can still lose the assets to a higher bid, and while stalking horse protections exist, getting outbid doesn’t create any operating value.

Let us assume that all goes well, though. I believe that this is where the move becomes more than strategic. It is now a capital allocation test. Why? Well, buying distressed assets can be a smart move, especially when you consider where QUBT is in its lifecycle, but it also introduces integration risks. It is also possible that the company is just accumulating complexity faster than it accumulates revenue.

At the same time, we must view these moves within a broader context. Luminar Technology’s (LAZRQ) Chapter 11 bankruptcy process is largely forced because it lost a major contract with Volvo, and it is also facing some leadership issues. That is exactly why the assets are available and why the purchase price is not the full price. If QUBT can complete this deal in addition to the initial semiconductor acquisition, then it is essentially getting crucial assets much cheaper than it otherwise would have. So, the onus is on it to retain the talent, stabilize customers, and integrate operations with its existing business. That is easier said than done, and I’ll keep an eye on how it goes.

When I step back, the monetization path is still simple in theory. QUBT needs to move from services to products, from pilots to repeat orders, and from validation to scale. The Luminar moves can accelerate that transition. They can also expose whether management can execute under pressure.

The Numbers Force The Conversation

Once I get past the technology and vision, the numbers still pull me back to reality. The fact is that QUBT does not have a revenue problem in the abstract. It has a revenue problem relative to its ambition and cost base, and that isn’t surprising at all.

Yes, revenue exists, but it is small and uneven. One better quarter like Q3 helps, but it does not change the shape of the business by itself, especially when we still don’t have visibility into the near-term revenue potential. Also, the (gross) margin is fairly solid, albeit it is far from stable too. It’s been as high as 55% and as low as 30% over the last three full fiscal years, so we can’t draw any reasonable inferences there either.

The real anchor is operating expenses. QUBT runs a business that currently costs around $10 million per quarter to operate. I understand why R&D is heavy, but that cost base sets the rules of the game, and the company is still nowhere near covering it through operations.

However, you might also see some “profitable” quarters like Q1 and Q3 FY25, and then you think the company is doing better than it actually is. That’s not the case, and those profits have typically come from non-operating items, especially fair value changes related to its derivative liabilities and interest income on a large cash balance. They’re just accounting tricks.

The balance sheet, though, is a different case entirely. QUBT has raised so much capital that I cannot countenance any near or medium-term financial stress. The company had around $555 million in cash and short-term investments at the end of Q3, and it raised another $750 million in October, so management has a lot of room to invest and experiment. I already touched on the Luminar deals, and it is likely that they’ll be looking at more bolt-on acquisitions over the next few years.

The thing is, that level of financial strength for such an operationally small business creates a new tension. The company has all the money it needs to keep running for a very long time, but now, it needs to prove it can use capital well. The questions on every investor’s lips are probably going to be along the lines of: Am I seeing movement toward products and repeat customers, or am I watching a well-funded R&D engine keep running?

That’s why the dilution matters so much here. Shareholders have already paid for the time, and what comes next for QUBT will determine whether they’ll get real value for that time.

What I’m Looking Out For

Looking ahead, I believe that QUBT’s story is all about the proof. For one, it would be nice if revenue stopped resetting every quarter. That’s unlikely in the near term, but it’s something to expect sooner than later, given the moves and current market opportunities for the company’s existing technology and infrastructure. That includes the TFLN foundry and Luminar Semiconductor acquisition. They give QUBT a path that doesn’t fully rely on whenever the broader market decides to adopt a quantum-based or supported future timeline.

Leading on from that, I expect operational spending to increase, given integration costs and other associated expenses. The only criterion for not judging the extra spending as wasteful is if it translates into clearer commercial signals. It is one thing to have money in the bank, and it is another to use it to improve traction. Given the context of the Luminar Semiconductor deal, I expect QUBT to do a good job of integrating that operating photonics capability and using it to build credibility and utilization. That’s where the revenue and accompanying upside will come from.

Valuation: This Is Still A Story Stock

As I write this, QUBT is trading at $11.75/share, and it is up ¬80% over the last year. Conversely, the stock price is just 10% higher than it was 5 years ago, when it was just getting into the quantum computing game. As you can tell, the traditional valuation tools don’t help much here. The revenue base is too small and too unstable, and the operating losses are still structural, so we still have to go back to the balance sheet.

As I’ve mentioned before, QUBT holds an unusually large pool of cash and investments relative to the business it operates today. That private placement last October raised around $750 million, selling 37 million shares to investors. With those numbers, the price/share came out to ¬$20, which is nearly twice today’s price. Yes, that liquidity lowers the near-term downside risk and gives management years of runway, but it also comes with major dilution risks. In practical terms, investors are paying for time, optionality, and long-term execution, not any near-term revenue potential.

The company's market cap is ~$2.8 billion, and its enterprise value is ~$2.25 billion. This is a business with under a million dollars in annual revenue, and with around $2.5 billion in debt, the leverage changes how I think about the risk. Say, for example, that QUBT manages to scale to $40-50 million in annual revenue over the next five years, and then up to $100 million in another two years after that. Today's $2.25 billion EV would imply that the EV/Sales multiple for the former range drops down to 45-56x, and at $100 million, around 23x. Those are extremely inflated premiums over the sector median of around 3x EV/Sales, so clearly, even the very optimistic commercialization scenarios are already heavily baked in at this price.

This is exactly why the stock sits in a valuation gray zone for me. If the foundry begins to attract repeat demand and product revenue compounds, then I think the valuation can grow into itself. Conversely, I think it’s reasonable to assume that the market will eventually stop paying for possibility and start anchoring to what the business earns if we don’t see more positive development around commercialization and revenue.

So I treat valuation here as a question of confidence. I’m not comfortable underwriting a Buy just yet, at least not without clearer traction. At the same time, I can’t call this a Sell because the floor for the stock is pretty high, given the balance sheet and the strategic leverage management has created for itself.

Risks And Catalysts

I'd say the biggest risk to QUBT at this point, is time. There's a lot of leverage on the balance sheet, so the company doesn't have the luxury of open-ended experimentation. The market is already pricing in a future where the photonics and foundry strategy works. So, it cannot afford to any drags in commercialization. If that happens, we will see the valuation compress really quickly.

Leading on from that is capital allocation. I've discussed the Luminar Semiconductor and asset acquisitions, and we need to see a steady cadence with integration. Any delays with that or translating these assets into revenue matters more now. With that debt on the balance sheet, it is crucial that any move the company makes helps it start making money.

Product-led revenue needs to scale, not just one-off services, and ideally, we'd start seeing some moves from the Luminar integration. I'm assuming that it starts recognizing decent revenue by 2026 or 2027, and that revenue needs to stay consistent from quarter-to-quarter. If that happens, and we see some growth on a bi-annual basis, then that would change how the market frames the business. With the foundry, if external customers begin to place recurring orders, then it is easier for me to defend the optionality in today's price.

I’m At A Hold

Financially speaking, QUBT is in a great place. What needs to happen next is clear progress on an execution front, across all of its business lines and acquisitions. I can see the appeal behind the stock, especially because it is a quantum computing mover, but I’d rather watch the story closely for a bit longer before dipping my toes into the water.
